I'm in favor of @HereticSK's suggestion in https://github.com/quantopian/alphalens/issues/253#issuecomment-361794546, to factor out a separate `create_factor_data` (though it would be nice to find a more descriptive name for this operation. Is there a better word we could use for "stacked dataframe of forward returns plus group and factor information") function and have the implementation of `get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns` be composed out of `get_forward_returns` and `create_factor_data`.

I think it's hard in general to make objective arguments about API design, but here's an attempt at a justification for why I prefer this design over adding another optional argument to `get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns`:

- Personally, I find it easier to understand an API with several functions that each have a clear purpose than an API with one giant "do everything" function.
- Any time a function has a conjunction in its name, my instinct is to break `do_x_and_y` into separate `do_x` and `do_y` functions.  A user can always write `do_x_and_y` themselves in terms of `do_x` and `do_y`, but they can't always extract just the `do_x` or the `do_y` part if that's all they want or need.
In some cases `do_x_and_y` is such a common pattern that it's worth having the builtin for convenience's sake (and typing convenience matters more for a library like alphalens where the expected user is doing interactive analysis than it would for something like a library providing low-level linear algebra primitives, say), but if we also provide `do_x` and `do_y` as separate builtins, then advanced users who care about performance can build exactly what they need out of the more primitive operations.

> Unfortunately, I'm not sure it's quite so simple... the delimiters need to be prefixed by the relevant comment mark of the language or else it's invalid syntax. 

The delimiter can be inside a comment, whatever it happens to be. I don't think the delimiter needs to be the *entire* comment. For example, these could all work:

```python
def function():
    ## **BEGIN SOLUTION**
    ## **END SOLUTION**
```
```scheme
(define function
    (lambda (v)
        ;; **BEGIN SOLUTION**
        ;; **END SOLUTION**
    )
)
```
> The comment mark is then something that would either need to be tracked explicitly by nbgrader itself, 

No, I wouldn't do that. I would just look for the text delimiters in a line, and use that whole line to indicate indentation level (starting with whatever starts that line).

> Also, although it is unusual, there are some languages (e.g. Mathematica) that only have support for block comments (rather than inline comments), which means it would not be sufficient to just prefix the delimeters with the comment mark.

Wouldn't this work:

```
some.other(language);
   like-mathematica...
        /* **BEGIN SOLUTION** */
        /* **END SOLUTION** */
```

I really don't like "'# YOUR CODE HERE\\nraise NotImplementedError()'" as a default, for a couple of reasons. One is that it does cause an error, which can be intimidating. Error messages take some practice to be able to read and understand. The other is that it is advanced Python, and Python-specific. I would try to keep defaults as neutral and simple as possible. But, at least it is configurable!

So doing an in memory stream of the zip file is not possible. The closest I got was having this logic, but the stream we get from s3 is not seekable so you cannot feed that into the ZipFile object:
```py
    try:
        response = s3.get_object(Bucket=bucket, Key=key)
        s3_object = response['Body']
        zippedfile = zip(s3_object)
        namelist = zippedfile.namelist()
        for filename in namelist:
            with zippedfile.open(filename) as f:
                s3.put_object(Bucket=dump_bucket, Key=filename, Body=f)

    except Exception as e:
        print(e)
        print('Error getting object {} from bucket {}. Make sure they exist and your bucket is in the same region as this function.'.format(key, bucket))
        raise e
```
After looking at your code, to lessen memory consumption you could avoid reading it all in memory by not calling the ``data = zfile.read(filename)``. You could possibly instead have logic such as:
```py
        namelist = zippedfile.namelist()
        for filename in namelist:
            with zippedfile.open(filename) as f:
                s3.put_object(Bucket=dump_bucket, Key=filename, Body=f)
```
Note that this will improve memory consumption but it may take longer to upload because this is a single threaded upload.

I was looking at the Lambda documentation and it seemed to indicate that you only have 500 MB of disk. That is why in my initial attempt I tried to avoid using ``download_file`` because that saves it all to disk (assuming the files we are downloading are greater than 500MB). wrote:

> the purpose of objects implementing the mapping interface is so people can
> write functions that work on dict's, and if you happen to have an instance
> instead, you can use the same function.
>
> Fair enough! The trouble is, right now the keys() etc. methods are not
> safe to use. They're not even used by Schematics itself because they would
> fail on any model that has a field with an overlapping name.
>
> So, if the methods are going to stay, then I think we should
>
>    - explicitly forbid any field names that would cause a conflict
>    - accurately define the iterators' semantics; e.g., should
>    serializables be included.
>
>
This seems like a solid idea to me. The list of fields is likely very short
and can be checked in the metaclass, meaning the overhead is negligible.


>
>
> In my days of building Brubeck (the web framework), the difference between
> using objects or functions for routing turned out to be roughly 33% gains
> when using just functions.
>
> You mean a scenario involving Schematics? If you still have the testcase,
> I'd love to get it for benchmarking.
>
First, this story is about Brubeck, not Schematics, but it helps you
understand why I prefer not using classes when I have the choice and why I
held it as a virtue of Schematics.

The story is that while building Brubeck, I initially took the class
approach to handlers that Tornado took. I then heard from Flask users that
they liked just putting a decorator on functions, so I added support for
that too.

Because everything else was the same, I was able to directly test the
performance of Tornado's class style vs. straight functions. In Tornado,
the handler is a class. Not a class instance, but a class. The instance is
created for each incoming request. Additional overhead is that the class
supports HTTP GET by implementing `get()`. For each request, the class is
checked to see if it has implemented the function that would exist for the
incoming HTTP method. HTTP POST = `post()`, etc.

We implemented the test with a straight-up hello world test. A very basic
request kept the differences between using functions and classes minimal.
The results was that functions would handle about 133 requests for every
100 the classes could do.

Further, the reason I first liked Schematics being as instance free as
possible, is that I could use Schematics models for building API endpoints
and could do so without instantiating any models. No handler classes and as
few Model classes as required to get the metaprogramming to work. For this
case, the dict was often API inputs and I'd validate / transform this data
based on what model the handler was built around.

It's been such a long time since I used Brubeck... I think I might've put
the results on my tumblr, which no longer exists.

Accessing plain dictionaries is obviously faster, but in the context of
> Schematics this only accounts for a fraction of the CPU costs. And any such
> performance difference could be optimized away by making the transformation
> loops aware of instances and having them access _data directly.
>
Anyone can put Schematics in a loop though.

> When importing, the resulting (internal) data structure is a
> newly-populated dictionary in any case. So literally the only difference
> between returning a dictionary vs. a model instance is the creation of the
> model object, which doesn't seem very expensive in the grand scheme of
> things.
>
> Hence, it's my belief that, if there are any notable performance gaps
> between plain dictionaries and model instances, they are merely design
> issues that can be fixed.
>
> One scenario where, historically, there has been a significant performance
> penalty is basic validation. Passing a dict to validate() is efficient
> because both conversion and validation are performed at once:
>
> validate(MyModel, inputdict)
>
> Doing the same thing using an instance involves importing first and
> validating after:
>
> MyModel(inputdict).validate()
>
> Since validation also has the conversion step baked in, the whole import
> process is run twice for no reason.
>
> This is addressed in PR #374
> <https://github.com/schematics/schematics/pull/374>, which improves the
> performance of the above scenario by ~40%. The functional variant is now
> only about 3% faster than creating and validating instances.
>
This is good news.

Memory consumption is another consideration when using instances. Python
classes are built on top of dict's, which all must have considerations on
top for growing memory structures, and all must have unique allocations.

Class instances, by nature, require more resources.

Alas, Python isn't the fastest language out there.

>

@thunderfish24 I appreciate your efforts to question and improve the API. Please see the following issues for the discussion behind the current design:

- #17 : initial ModelChain, PVSystem, and refactored Location design. Discussion of LocalizedPVSystem vs. PVSystem + Location + ModelChain.
- #93 : implementation of ideas in #17.
- #194 : refactor of ModelChain to use methods instead of inheritance. Includes some supporting material for why I chose to change the approach.

The [modeling paradigms](http://pvlib-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/package_overview.html#modeling-paradigms) section of the documentation attempts to summarize the philosophy, though I can't say that it fully captures my thoughts or the community's.

With a few years of use, development, and maintenance I can add the following. 

1. I continue to mostly like the design philosophy of ``PVSystem``, and ``Location``.  Intrinsic attributes of a system or location are stored as object attributes. Extrinsic things that influence the system or location are passed into the method arguments.
2. ``ModelChain`` works surprisingly well. It avoids inheritance issues. It supports user extensions that do not require modifying the source code. 
2. It's difficult to add new models to ``ModelChain``, even when the model API fits well with existing ``ModelChain`` code. This is in part understandable because I do think tying together all of the modeling steps is just a hard problem. The inference and getter/setter code adds another level of complication for developers, but they do make it easier for end users to work with the class.
2. The ``ModelChain`` and even the ``Location/PVSystem`` API is a poor fit for some use cases. That's ok.
2. Because of the immediately above point, it's critical that pvlib continues to maintain a strong, well-tested "base layer" of functions that implement the models. These functions will never go out of style.
2. New frameworks, such as dask, have been developed in the last few years that provide powerful alternatives to ``ModelChain``. pvlib should *not* build those frameworks into its code. pvlib should add examples of how to use its functions with those frameworks.
2. The ``PVSystem`` class needs to live in its own module. This would make it easier to work with the pure functions.
2. ``Location`` only implements ``get_*`` and ``from_*`` wrappers. We should consider the same approach for ``PVSystem``.
2. ``LocalizedPVSystem`` is a disaster. Multiple inheritance is extremely hard to do well. In my view, we should either go all in on a single class that only implements high level ``get_*`` style wrapper methods, or we should delete ``LocalizedPVSystem`` entirely. I don't want to do the former, and I think a few people would push back against the latter, so the likely outcome is that nothing changes in the foreseeable future.

The main problem is that because CMake has improved so much, and because C++11 support, CUDA support, targets, etc. has improved, that many packages are now requiring newer CMake, and all users simply have to install a new CMake. I believe part of the problem is that systems viewed CMake 3.0 like Python 3.0, which is not the case.

For completeness, since I keep mentioning that new CMake installs are easy:

```bash
pip install --user cmake
```
will give you a modern CMake on almost any system that supports pip. A local install of CMake on Linux can be obtained with 
```bash
mkdir cmake && wget -qO- "https://cmake.org/files/v3.9/cmake-3.9.2-Linux-x86_64.tar.gz" | tar --strip-components=1 -xz -C cmake
export PATH=`pwd`/cmake/bin:$PATH
```

Unfortunately, the `VirtualMachine` REST API which we use only gives the reference:

```python
vm = compute_client.virtual_machines.get('vm1', 'vm1')

```

```
import requests

def get_token_from_client_credentials(endpoint, client_id, client_secret):
    payload = {
        'grant_type': 'client_credentials',
        'client_id': client_id,
        'client_secret': client_secret,
        'resource': 'https://management.core.windows.net/',
    }
    response = requests.post(endpoint, data=payload).json()
```

Looking great :) I wonder if we can go even further in simplifying it for the user. These are three ways I imagine using it.

```python
from cartoframes import Examples

# 1. current implementation
df = Examples().read_taxi()

# 2. desired additional usage - loaders are functions in the module
# this feels more natural to me / requires less of the user
from cartoframes import examples
df = examples.read_taxi()

# or
from cartoframes.examples import read_taxi
df = read_tax()

# 3. super users
# this is currently possible
example_context = Examples()

# but it would be easier if the user didn't have to instantiate it
# if they want to use the special cartocontext, they can just import it
from cartoframes.examples import example_context

df = example_context.query('''
    SELECT s.*, count(t.*)
    FROM taxi as t
    JOIN ny_census_tract as s
    ON st_intersects(t.the_geom, s.the_geom)
    ''')
```

The first method is nice and easy to do with the current implementation. To me, having to create the class instance seems like a step that we don't necessarily need the user to go through. To support the second and third options, we could offer a pre-made context like this in `examples.py`:

```python
"""examples.py"""

example_context = Examples()

# and define the loading methods as functions instead
def load_taxi(limit=None):
    return example_context.read('taxi_dataset', limit=limit)
```

And we could re-use these functions in the class:

```python

class Examples(CartoContext):
    def __init__():
        pass

    def read_taxi(self, limit=None):
        return load_taxi(limit=limit)
```

For information, if Impyla is run against Hive and timeout error occurs, retrying an executeStatement is not useful, as HiveServer2 behavior is to automatically cleanup a session on a disconnect.  Thus the retried executeStatement will get :

"org.apache.hive.service.cli.HiveSQLException: Invalid SessionHandle: SessionHandle"

In fact, the implicit retry is harmful in this case as it makes it so that the application has no indication that the error happened and the need to open a new session.

```
In [1]: import attr
```

```python
        self.loop = loop

class LoopTester(object):
    def __init__(self, loop=None):
        if loop is None:
            loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        self.loop = loop
        self.counter = 0
        asyncio.ensure_future(self.run(), loop=self.loop)

    async def run(self):
        while self.loop.is_running():
            print('basic test %d' % (self.counter))
            self.counter += 1
            await asyncio.sleep(1)

if __name__ == '__main__':

    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)
    loop.set_debug(False)

    ws = WebServer(loop=loop)

        loop.run_forever()
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        tasks = asyncio.gather(
                    *asyncio.Task.all_tasks(loop=loop),
                    loop=loop,
                    return_exceptions=True)
        tasks.add_done_callback(lambda t: loop.stop())
        tasks.cancel()
```

Well.
The reason is: starting from python 3.5.3 `asyncio.get_event_loop()` returns not *global loop* but currently executed one if the function is called from a coroutine.
`aiojobs.create_scheduler()` is a corotine.
So `get_event_loop()` is always correct for the case.

P.S.
Explicit loop passing was very important prior to the fix but now there is the consensus that explicit loops should be gone from public APIs. While libraries could keep the reference internally for sake of performance.

P.P.S.
I am `asyncio` co-maintainer BTW, I know how to work with it quite well :)

FYI: I removed the `priority:1` label. I know we haven't defined these priorities well yet. But I would like to use them in this fashion:

- `priority:1`: **Must fix now!** Bug or deficiency preventing the proper installation or runtime of NuPIC for most users.
- `priority:2`: **Must fix soon.** Bug that prevents proper usage of NuPIC for a minority of users. New feature that is important to work on soon, or that is blocking the progress of other issues.
- `priority:3`: **Normal priority.** Things that should be done, but nothing pressing.
- `priority:4`: **Low priority.** Work that would be "nice to have", but might never get done and no one would care that much.

Could you please give me some insight into your use-case for this? From my perspective it is more or less confusing that there are .template files which are implicitly threaded as json. There for I would like to understand your problems here.

alembic's autogenerate render does not currently render the info dictionary.   SQLA itself doesn't use .info at all so is not part of what it needs to render column DDL.  However, if redshift and other dialects are relying upon info for extra DDL hints, that is a supported case so alembic should support rendering .info.   A fix for this though should include .info for everything, columns, tables, indexes, etc.   It also should be careful not to barf if there are un-reprable things in that .info dictionary (as any application can put any kind of object in this dictionary), and perhaps even only render simple ints, strings and booleans from the dictionary.    this would take place in alembic/autogenerate/render.py and of course be exercised in new unit tests in tests/test_autogen_render.py.

I would note that alembic allows user-defined rendering right now, which would allow redshift to supply a workaround if this was worth it, but it would have to define the whole Column render itself: http://alembic.zzzcomputing.com/en/latest/api/runtime.html#alembic.runtime.environment.EnvironmentContext.configure.params.render_item  probably not worth it.

> @waltaskew can you stick with two separate table.create() Insert.from_select queries for now? Otherwise see if you can get sqlalchemy core to provide an API we can implement

Yeah, should be possible to dynamically build a table object off of a selectable's `.columns` (as long as the query was built off of column objects rather than just column names as strings) and do a `table.create()` in a transaction with the inserts. It looks like Redshift does support doing DDL in a transaction.

This does seem like a slightly weird omission from the SQLAlchemy API.

I noticed that [according to Leaflet](http://leafletjs.com/reference-1.2.0.html#control-layers-l-control-layers), any additional base layers should not be added to the map. In Folium all base layers are added to the map. As a result the last base layer is selected in layer control, while the first base layer is displayed.

Fixing this by not adding additional base layers to the map is hard, since we then need to check this in every class that inherits from `Layer`. Removing the additional base layers in `LayerControl` also works, so I added it here.

> No, we still need to store a list of classes for each name.
However, we don't need to store duplicate values in the lists.

When storing serializer class in the registry, the module name is used to disambiguate between _same_ classes, where _same_ is defined by the fullpath (module+classname) [there](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/class_registry.py#L48).

However when dealing with fullpath [just under](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/class_registry.py#L55), that would mean that each new entry is understood as _same_ as an existing one. Therefore we don't need a list there anymore... Or we do need a way to differentiate between different classes that does not rely on their fullpath (which might not be suitable for complexity cost).

@jhitze I think your solution assumes that the transformation is just mapping between names, but @justanr is indicating that he needs to customize how field values are processed depending on which interface he is dumping to.

This is an interesting use case. It reminds me of services like Stripe that consume a 3rd party API and expose it as a Stripe API. Although your use case sounds mostly pass through, you are still relying on some amount of processing between the APIs assuming your schema is enforcing type validation.

|   | Legacy API |   | Processing Layer |   | New API |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **⇝** | respond A<sub>o</sub> | load A<sub>o</sub> to B<sub>i</sub> | process | dump B<sub>o</sub> to C<sub>i</sub> | receive C<sub>i</sub> |
| **⇜** | receive A<sub>i</sub> | dump B<sub>o</sub> to A<sub>i</sub> | process | load C<sub>o</sub> to B<sub>i</sub> | respond C<sub>o</sub> |

This diagram represents 2 schemas that have the intermediate schema representation (B) in common.

Your use case seems to assume that the processing layer (B) will use the same naming convention as the new API (C), so the second schema feels redundant.

Classic Big Mac vs. Two Cheese Burgers dilemma :stuck_out_tongue_winking_eye:.

[![Classic Big Mac vs. Two Cheese Burgers dilemma](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/800/1*fwnk-2uYUdM0ByaIze3HCQ.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@diegooliveirasnchez/which-is-better-a-big-mac-or-2-cheeseburgers-4351382443a8#.3wpoylb1q)

I think you are describing a serialization pipeline that separates data processing from schema naming. I am imagining a SchemaInterface class that represents one side of a schema naming and a SchemaPipeline that generates the intermediate Schemas from a sequence of SchemaInterfaces. A SchemaInterfaceMapping would have to be created to associate the field names and transform the data.

This would allow you to define one interface for your legacy API and one interface for your new API. The second interface would be reused for the processing layer, and a mapping would be created between the legacy interface and the processing interface.

---

That might be a more generic solution than you were looking for, but I think the solution probably requires some abstraction on top of Schema that knows how to reuse a common set of fields to construct two different schemas.

Indeed, adding attributes dynamically is a risky approach, and not ideal. Here is my suggested approach:

1. Represent alternatives derived from optional parameters with the built-in `Union` type. E.g., the type of `def foo(x: int, y: int = 0) -> str` should be `Union[Callable[[int], str], Callable[[int, int], str]]`.
2. Modify `handle_call` (or related) to handle either a single callable or a union of callables.
3. Modify `type_store.py` to handle this representation as well. Note that they use a decorator to signify different signatures. This will take more work, but if all goes well, you can modify the type of the `functions` and `methods` dictionary attributes to have just single types, rather than lists of types, which is very nice.

Thanks.  Here's what I'm finding.

The pattern filter is working exactly as expected.  Only logstash-* indices are kept.

Then it begins with the age filter, using creation date, and keeping everything older than 5 days from right now.  That is in epoch time: 1476037644, which is visible in the log file as the `point of reference, (1476037644)`.  Converted to a readable format, that looks like `Sun, 09 Oct 2016 18:27:24 GMT`.

So let's look at one that you would think would be excluded from the actionable list: `logstash-2016.10.11`. Clearly, an index with a date stamp only 3 days in the past should be removed, and it is.  But the date stamp should surprise you:

```
Removed from actionable list: Index "logstash-2016.10.11" age (1476443012), direction: "older", point of reference, (1476037644)
```
When I convert `1476443012` to a readable format, it says: `Fri, 14 Oct 2016 11:03:32 GMT`.  Wait.  That index says it was created at 11:03 GMT, which is roughly 7 hours ago.  The `creation_date` was only seven hours ago, not the expected number of days in the past.

Let's take one that should have remained in the actionable list and try the same process.

```
Removed from actionable list: Index "logstash-2016.10.03" age (1476443142), direction: "older", point of reference, (1476037644)
```

The age, based on `creation_date` here, is `1476443142 `, which translates to a readable date of `Fri, 14 Oct 2016 11:05:42 GMT`

What's going on is that whatever is creating these indices is doing it all at once, and it only started about 7 to 8 hours ago.

Now, if you want to change the number of replicas still on these indices, use `name` instead of `creation_date`.  Your age filter would look like:

```
- filtertype: age
  source: name
  timestring: %Y.%m.%d
  direction: older
  unit: days
  unit_count: 5
```

You don't need to have `exclude` there if it is empty.  This should yield the expected result because it calculates age based on the timestamp in the index name, not when Elasticsearch created the index.

The bottom line is that Curator cannot work with AWS ES because AWS ES does not support the `/_cluster/state/metadata` endpoint (see #685).  There is an AWS forum link in #685, and even a comment from one of the core AWS ES developers.

How did this happen?  Well, the `get_client` method is largely carried over from Curator 3.x, and some PRs were submitted to add AWS IAM functionality there.  Curator 4 was developed to support _only_ Elasticsearch 2.0 and higher.  At the time, AWS ES was only on version 1.5, so it was not compatible at all.  After Curator 4 was released, then Amazon released their 2.x version, and it did not support the `/_cluster/state/metadata` endpoint, which Curator 4 depends on.

I am improving the documentation in #771 to reflect these things.  I apologize for the inconvenience.

This ticket is being closed as a duplicate of #685

Regarding Matt's questions: 
(1) One problem with the model is that for large documents, the GPU will not have enough memory. One way to ensure things fit is to take random chunks of the document. Suppose you have a document x with n words: [x1, x2, ..., xn]. On the first iteration, you might use only x[5:5 + c], on the second iteration, you might use only x[22:22 + c]. This makes sure you eventually all of your data.
(2) Correct, variational dropout is only at the context layer.
(3) The tricky part is that this constraint enforcing happens in the middle of the computation graph, since it decides which span gets pruned. You can technically do this on pure Python relatively simply, but it would be quite slow. In reality, this doesn't affect the performance, but it is needed to use the official evaluation metrics. 
(4) Yes this is quite simple to do, I just haven't gotten to it yet.
(5) Sorry, the way I presented it was confusing. The lack of ensembling isn't contributing to the gap that I mentioned. It just something that's missing.

@techalchemy Describing "4 years ago" as "just" is a postitively stdlib-esque perception of the passage of time ;) (Not that I disagree, it's just a different perspective on time scales from the one that drives a lot of developer tooling)

As for whether we'll ever bundle `pipenv` with the stdlib there's actually two answers to that based on timeline:

1. Right now, the answer is "No", for simple reasons of maturity - both `pipenv` and the `Pipfile` and `Pipfile.lock` formats are still very new, and we'd want all of them to be clearly stable before promoting them from "recommended in a packaging.python.org tutorial" to "installed by default with the reference interpreter".
2. Longer term, exactly what we do will depend in part on the exact form that `pip`'s native `Pipfile` and `Pipfile.lock` support ends up taking (see https://github.com/pypa/pipfile/issues/108 for more on that), and also on whether or not we can get the `pip install pipenv` UX to the "it just works" level (see https://github.com/pypa/python-packaging-user-guide/issues/396 and https://github.com/pypa/python-packaging-user-guide/issues/394 for some discussion of the problems both with getting installed scripts to work reliably in general, as well as with the reasons why `pipenv` isn't a full replacement for direct use of `pip`)

The essential question anyone proposing this feature should answer is *why*. Due to the locking nature of the Pipfile-lockfile combo, every section added would induce considerable increase of difficulty in implementation because Pipenv would need to consider the interaction between sections. It is therefore not enough to propose this based simply on aesthetics; you need to propose a real, practical, insurmountable need for this feature to convince the developers to consider it.

(Or, alternatively, you can implement it yourself, promise to shepherd the feature and fix problems it introduces.)

Expanding on @bertjwregeer's excellent point about `--no-download`, the issue here is that even if we pinned setuptools in our `requirements.txt` we would still be broken because the `virtualenv` package installed the latest setuptools already which broke the virtualenv. We would not be able to downgrade to our pinned version after that. Secondly, `pip freeze` **by default** excludes setuptools making it that much harder and non-obvious to pin it - **even when** a package in your tree depends on setuptools explicitly for runtime pkg_resources features. In this new age of setuptools bumping the major version number frequently it's possible these defaults should be re-evaluated to make things easier to pin and to create more stable environments without requiring things like `VIRTUALENV_NO_DOWNLOAD=1` and `pip freeze --all`.

目前已经有框架级别的持久化支持，已经稳定运行很长时间了。

开源版本内置了一个简单的 DiskPersistProvider ，可以将需要的持久化的数据写到本地磁盘上。

如果需要，可以在自定义 mod 中设置 config.base.persist = True，然后添加 persist_provider 。

``` python
from rqalpha.utils.disk_persist_provider import DiskPersistProvider

persist_provider = DiskPersistProvider(persist_path)
env.set_persist_provider(persist_provider)
```

Hi @tanaysoni, streamparse already supports this by allowing you to pass Storm configuration options via your topology definition. I'll admit better documentation here is definitely needed but here's a quick example to get you over the hump.

A list of all Storm config options is available [here](https://storm.apache.org/javadoc/apidocs/backtype/storm/Config.html) but the options you're looking for are likely [WORKER_CHILDOPTS](https://storm.apache.org/javadoc/apidocs/backtype/storm/Config.html#WORKER_CHILDOPTS), or [TOPOLOGY_WORKER_CHILDOPTS](https://storm.apache.org/javadoc/apidocs/backtype/storm/Config.html#TOPOLOGY_WORKER_CHILDOPTS).

From the docs, sounds as though TOPOLOGY_WORKER_CHILDOPTS is the one to use when submitting topologies and that WORKER_CHILDOPTS is probably best managed from your `storm.yaml`.

To set this param on a streamparse deploy, you can do something like:

```
> sparse submit -n my_topology -o worker.topology_worker_childopts "-Xms<size> -Xmx<size>"
```

Not 100% sure that `worker.topology_worker_childopts` will work, give it a shot and let me know.

Hi @vsoch,

Sorry to hear you're having a hard time. I took a look at the errors you're getting and glanced at the code you linked to. A couple of things to get started:

Native app clients should definitely be able to use "localhost" redirect URIs with either `http` or `https`. You don't even need to explicitly add them when configuring the client, you just get to use them automatically. For an example you can check out https://github.com/globus/native-app-examples/blob/master/example_local_server.py and see that it uses `http://localhost` as its redirect.

I'm not sure what all you have going on in the "workaround" version of your flask app but I suspect the `invalid_grant` error message is because you're somehow reusing the authorization code in a new "flow" and the OAuth2 PKCE check is failing. The native app grant is special in that to get around not having a client secret it generates one-time challenges per auth flow and that challenge gets associated with the auth code that's returned after a successful authentication. If you take a code and plug it into a new flow I suspect it will blow up on you.

Also, I noticed you're attaching data, specifically the globus sdk client instances, to Flask's `app` object. That data will persist across multiple requests so trying to do authorization flows with "global" state like that is going to be problematic--it may very well be the source of your problem. If you want to stash per-request data somewhere, checkout `g` (`from Flask import g`).

Anyway, I'd like to focus on getting your app working with the `localhost` redirect as it seems that was your preference. If you could set up your app to do that again (and hopefully take care of the stashing data on `app` issue) we can try and troubleshoot that.

It isn't obvious that this was broken before, but it definitely is now (since d26814c). The library cannot know if a path segment is supposed to include relative sub-parts like `outer/middle/inner` or if that should be % encoded as `outer%2fmiddle%2finner` - it can only deal with what it is given. 

The repeat encoding/decoding functionality is not a bug - it is correct implementation of the percent encoding and decoding rules. Only the calling code can know how many times it has been called. I think it is putting too much cleverness into `URL` to try to make it idempotent in the way this issue calls for, though I understand that it seems like an elegant solution. Instead I think if an application is calling `u.asIRI().asIRI()...` that is almost certainly a usage error, and if it is not a usage error, it's in the calling code's domain to know that.

Quite separately, I believe the effect of `_textcheck()` here, which throws if path segments include percent encoded reserved characters is a bug. There's simply no problem having percent encoded characters in a URI or IRI. That's what they're for.

Apologies in advance if I've misunderstood the intent of this issue, it's a complex area and I've probably made some mistakes understanding it all!

This probably isn't news to you, but just in case you or someone looking at this later doesn't know, if you are in a connexion endpoint you shouldn't even need to json.dumps() it. You could just return it directly (```return xx.tod_dict()```), and connexion will take care of dumping it and setting the JSON headers for you. Or if it was a normal flask endpoint, you could do ```return jsonify(xx.to_dict())``` to make sure the headers properly get set.

It seems to me that there are several causes of this problem:
 * ResponseValidator stores only one mimetype from spec and does not check actual response mimetype
 * Operation.get_mimetype returns only 1 mimetype from `produces` list. If there are several entries, they are discarded and json is returned (https://github.com/zalando/connexion/blob/master/connexion/operation.py#L333)

Please apply this last change:
```patch
--- /home/travis/build/zalando/connexion/connexion/decorators/produces.py:before	2017-02-12 12:47:19.333433
+++ /home/travis/build/zalando/connexion/connexion/decorators/produces.py:after	2017-02-12 12:50:03.352099
@@ -2,9 +2,9 @@
 import datetime
 import functools
 import logging
-import six
 
 import flask
+import six
 from flask import json
 
 from .decorator import BaseDecorator
```

Probably getting ahead of myself but dask grid search cv will likely also need to be modified to allow for delayed fit methods.  This could be desireable for the new bagging estimator

>  I wonder if there is something we could do to reduce dependence on this parameter? For example I wonder if eta might be definable per-row in some way

I've looked into this, and Hyperband can be applied to black-box algorithms that take some dataset as input, which is the API that scikit-learn implements. This would call `fit` and treat dataset size as the scarce resource instead of optimization iterations.

I think reworking the implementation will be a medium-length task, days not weeks. It can give real advantages including 

* being compatible with all sklearn models
* use of dask-searchcv (aka performance benefits)
* better cross validation
* much simpler code

Any preference on which way to go? I am inclined to go with this new approach, even though it's more work (and I am kicking myself for not realizing that Hyperband could be applied like this earlier).

Is the problem with `Incremental.fit` then? Should calling that "reset" the underlying estimator?

I was playing with some other meta-estimators from scikit-learn. It looks like we may be making a bad promise that the `Incremental.estimator` is actually the estimator trained.

For `sklearn.model_selection.GridSearchCV`, the `estimator` is not actually fit. It's cloned and then fit. Doing the same for `Incremental` would nicely solve this `warm_start` problem, and the problem of state leaking between `Incremental.fit` calls.

So my proposal is to

1. Remove the (implicit or explicit) promise that `Incremental.estimator` is actually fit
2. Change `Incremental.fit` to `clone` before calling fit
3. Maybe add an `estimator_` or `fitted_estimator_` attribute that has the actually trained estimator. This is analogous to `GridSearchCV.best_estimator_`

We'll also need to modify `Incremental.partial_fit` to be different from `Incremental.fit`, as it should *not* clone first.

Does that make any sense at all?

Okay, I tried things out. After a couple other problems, Dask-kubernetes was quite happy to start a bunch of workers on a remote Kubernetes cluster. However Dask itself is definitely not happy: nothing happens, and nothing shows up in the Dask dashboard.

My somewhat informed guess is that the Dask scheduler can't contact the Dask workers. And since there's no security between those two pieces (right?) I don't want to open a bunch of ports on the Kubernetes cluster so they can talk.

Sounds like the easiest solution would indeed be to have the scheduler running on another pod in the same cluster... (There's no need for direct communication between the dask client and the workers, right?)

> However this might become problematic if scale_down blocks (does it? I'm not that knowledgable about the kubernetes API). If so then we might want to offload this to a thread in a thread pool.

It's not an async call but it does not wait for kubernetes to actually delete the pods to return. It's just making a transaction with kubernetes API to schedule the pod deletion on its side as far as I understand. Maybe @yuvipanda knows better.

Still offloading looks simple to implement so we can use it to make it more cooperative with the event loop in case of slow interaction with the kubernetes API.

@jozsi Thanks for raising this issue! 
 
**_Authorization service implementation details_**
 
Putting aside SDK implementation for now, the authorization code provisioned via https://login.live.com/oauth20_authorize.srf enables access to specific resources or scopes e.g., bingads.manage or wl.signin. You can (again, without the SDK) request an authorization code for multiple resources e.g., scope=bingads.manage%20wl.signin; however, when you request access tokens only one scope is allowed e.g., scope=bingads.manage and the returned access token is only valid for that scope. When you request access tokens via https://login.live.com/oauth20_token.srf the scope query parameter is optional, since the authorization service remembers the scope or scopes that you used to get the authorization code. It is important to note that if multiple scopes were used initially to acquire the authorization code, and then later you do not specify the scope parameter via the access token request, the authorization service default to the first scope originally requested by your app. For example if you requested the authorization code with scope=wl.signin%20bingads.manage, and then did not set the scope for the token request, the returned access token will not be valid for Bing Ads API access (and no refresh token would be returned by default for the wl.signin scope). 
 
**_How might this apply to the SDK issue you observed?_** 
 
Its important to mention that the Bing Ads SDKs do not currently support multiple scopes i.e., [internally the SDK sets scope=bingads.manage](https://github.com/BingAds/BingAds-Python-SDK/blob/cb0509ebc7e20b79c8bae99f437d081063fb236b/bingads/authorization.py#L608) for authorization code requests, and we do not allow any scope to be set when requesting access tokens. I am making an educated guess in your case that some users granted your app access for another scope first e.g. wl.basic or wl.signin. Then when you attempt to request access tokens by refresh token the SDK a) does not set the scope to bingads.manage, and therefore lets the authorization service default to the original scope e.g., wl.basic or wl.signin and b) does not gracefully handle an empty refresh_token in the response. I can repro the same error that you observed under these conditions. 
 
Instead of depending on a variable default scope, probably the SDK should internally set scope=bingads.manage when requesting access tokens e.g., [here](https://github.com/BingAds/BingAds-Python-SDK/blob/cb0509ebc7e20b79c8bae99f437d081063fb236b/bingads/authorization.py#L427). You might prefer this as a workaround in the meantime. For now as a best practice I suggest that you use the SDK end-to-end and only request access tokens via the authorization code that was provisioned via the SDK in the first place i.e., where scope=bingads.manage (only).

There are more than we might expect, particularly in the exposure submodule, which do need the assumed float range.  Probably also color conversion.

It is worth noting that there are many other instances which will prove difficult to detect and correct - as some of our algorithms' parameters are dependent on the image range.

1) Some features will technically work, but with defaults that could now be orders of magnitude off.  This is a usability and accessibility issue.
2) Similarly, there are potentially serious public facing reproducibility concerns.  A particular set of defaults will no longer produce the same results in the same code.

Not trying to rain on any parades; I do see the benefits of letting sleeping floats lie.  But the behavior of using img_as_float as an input type AND range sanitizer is extremely long-standing and ingrained.  

Would it perhaps be better to retain the auto-scaling as default behavior, with the option to disable it for those who wish, or are unsatisfied with our current behavior?  Alternatively, could we satisfy those who want to return to a standard range by providing a method where they can pass their image and we return the exact scaling factor which will be applied, so they can return the result to the same space?

Because information is being lost, it's an important warning. When it is returned, our datatype conversion routine was called in a way such that the conversion will be (or at least has the potential to be) lossy.

A lot of users will encounter this warning, because many if not most of our algorithms operate using floating-point images. Usually for output/saving, these images must be converted back into integer formats. At that point the warning is raised, because all of the decimal values had to be compressed into 255 bins.

That said, the majority of the time people see this and either know what's going on - so the warning is just annoying and they'd prefer never to see it again - or fear something went wrong, when everything is fine and we really just wanted to give the user a FYI. Right now we probably warn a little too much.

Ok so I don't know enough about the depths of PIL but it seems to me that the error is either there or in the image itself. The problem is that PIL sees two frames in the image, the actual image, and an empty one behind it. [Here's my full exploration using pdb](https://gist.github.com/jni/f6345843a9fcc37c7eecf9a4f55bc8d3), but here's the executive summary:

```python
In [9]: from PIL import Image

In [10]: im = Image.open('156.jpg')

In [11]: im.n_frames
Out[11]: 2
```

Then, [this line](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/io/_plugins/pil_plugin.py#L123) tries to concatenate all the frames together. When the frames aren't the same size (in this case, `frames[0]` has shape `(978, 2000, 3)`, while `frames[1]` has shape `()`), you get an array of dtype `O`. The image is actually at `im[0]`.

So, as a first workaround, I suggest using `im = io.imread('156.jpg', img_num=0)`. That should give you the result you want. In the future, I see four options:

- don't fix, consider this a corrupted image
- raise upstream with PIL
- explicitly handle the case of an empty final frame and discard it
- proceed with dismantling our entire IO infrastructure and handing it off to imageio, which handles this image correctly (though, I suspect, at the expense of other, actually multi-frame images).

With that, we could make a small server-side plugin to ecrecover and reject register requests which fails this validation. OTOH, enforcing that on the server may limit our federation capabilities (we would need to ensure that federating servers also follow these rules for users registering with them). Anyway, it would be only a kind of account spam protection. I think the best approach for actual security is as it is now: any user in any server can send messages, and each node/client will check and validate that signature in the `displayName` matches the address that must be in the `userId` upon receiving the messages. This is done only in the matrix transport, and therefore, doesn't _tie_ Raiden to matrix protocol. Also, it'll continue allowing any compatible matrix server to federate the rooms, keeping decentralization capabilities at maximum.

I may be terribly off her, but I want to reiterate on what I wrote on #276:

Before I dive into the details, here are the preconditions to all my reflections:
1) a channel consists of two channel-end states
2) the state changes to a channel-end state are *received* transfers
3) each channel-end has a **strictly increasing** balance, that is, at any later state, the end-state's balance can only be higher
4) at settlement, the payout is calculated by netting the two channel end states and calculate an according final balance for the two ends

So:
I do not understand why any valid dispute should be allowed to contain `our_transfer` (i.e. a self-signed message). If we open up disputes to contain self-signed messages, then disputes will likely be never settled. Allowing self-signed messages allows manipulating the state of the other end.

Furthermore, I believe that containing `our_transfer` in the `close(our_transfer, their_transfer)` function is **only** a courtesy shortcut of saving the channel partner an on-chain transaction where they must provide their latest received transfer. I think the more generic name here leads to confusion as `close(our, their)` is actually a special case and the default case is `closeSingleTransfer(their_transfer)`.

I believe the default case for closing is this:
1) Alice writes her latest *received* balance to her channel end state.
2) Bob writes his latest *received* balance to his channel end state.
3) net balance is calculated and payed out from the overall deposits.

As an optimization, we allow Alice to provide her latest transfer **sent** to Bob, when calling `close(alices_transfer, bobs_transfer)`. If she does not lie about `alices_transfer`, then Bob does not need to write his own state himself. If she lies, he can call `disputeTransfer(alices_transfer)` in order to prove that he received a later (signed) state change for his channel-end state.

`closeWithoutTransfer()` should be only a special case for `closeSingleTransfer(None)`, which allows a participant to signal the channel that no transfer was **received**. By writing this state, the calling participant, say Alice, agrees that her channel end state balance was **never increased**. Now the other participant, Bob, is free to prove to the contract whatever balance he has received (and signed!) from Alice. After netting, the contract should pay out as much as Alice signed (up to the overall deposit).

Note, that a mismatch between overall deposit and the provided *received* balances can always happen, since either party can freely chose not to provide the latest (ever increasing) channel-end state to the contract, when closing. But: no party should ever be allowed to write updates to the other ends.

I'm not sure I agree with the approach here. Suppose I were to create a shell script to do this; it would likely look like this:
```
#!/bin/bash
_CONDA_ROOT=<whatever>
. "$_CONDA_ROOT/etc/profile.d/conda.sh"
conda activate $1
shift
exec $@
```
The `conda activate` involves a call to `conda shell.posix` and the sourcing of activate scripts. So we have the surrounding bash call, a single python call, and then the final exec.

In this case, you have the python call for `conda run` itself, which then calls a programmatically generated shell script, which in turn makes three more python calls, and then _returns_ for the final exec.

Seems like a better chain of events is this. From _within_ the `conda run` call itself, call the methods invoked by `conda shell.posix hook` and `conda shell.posix activate`. Build a script that includes those outputs as well as the command to be executed itself. Then send _that_ bash call to `execve`. That way, you're back to a single Python call and a single bash call.

Or perhaps we save ourselves the trouble and just ship the above script, and call it `conda-run`?

For people reading this unfamiliar with that setgid bit

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Setuid

> Setting the `setgid` permission on a directory ("`chmod g+s`") 
>
> 1. causes new files and subdirectories created within it to inherit its group ID, rather than the primary group ID of the user who created the file (the owner ID is never affected, only the group ID).
> 2. Newly created subdirectories inherit the `setgid` bit. Thus, this enables a shared workspace for a group without the inconvenience of requiring group members to explicitly change their current group before creating new files or directories.
> 3. only affects the group ID of new files and subdirectories created after the `setgid` bit is set, and is not applied to existing entities.
> 4. does not affect group ID of the files that are created elsewhere and moved to the directory in question. The file will continue to carry the group ID that was effected when and where it was created.

> For instance, conda packages should nominally specify their minimal set of dependencies. If there is functionality that requires additional packages, that can be offered in the documentation, and the code should be written in such a manner as to detect the presence of those dependencies and fail gracefully if they are absent.

I agree with the principle here, but I don't think it really works in practice.  I think there are a lot of cases where a package is not strictly a dependency, yet it's something that will be wanted by 80% or 90% of users of another package. Asking each one of those users to discover and install the recommended packages separately is a big burden on them, and requires a lot of human communication via documentation at a time when people are not likely to be reading it (when they are first trying things out and getting started, not diving deep).  What will most likely happen in those cases is that the "recommended" packages just won't get installed, and then users will miss out on features that most people will want.  Yet if those recommended packages are listed as hard dependencies, then there is *no* way that the software can be installed without them, which can be a major problem for some subset of users (when there are licensing issues, as for Qt with matplotlib, when e.g. some packages aren't available on certain platforms, due to lack of binaries, or just because the dependencies are really big).  NumPy, for instance, can be installed with no dependencies, yet most users will want the associated numerical libraries, and if it's installed without them they may give up on NumPy because they think it's too slow.  So it would be really great if recommended packages could be installed by default, unless overridden explicitly to obtain a minimal installation.

Yes, you can always solve this problem with metapackages, but that's a very heavyweight solution, multiplying the number of packages greatly. I do think it's the right approach for Matplotlib, which is already splitting into separate packages to avoid requiring Qt, but it seems like quite a burden on package maintainers in general, with the predictable result that they'll usually keep the 80% of users happy while making life very painful for the other 20%.

1. add conda/bin to PATH (old way)
2. add conda/exec to PATH (new folder, contains only activate and conda as symlinks to those in the bin folder)

This was discussed in #864, with the answer being i.e. "`no_softlinks` should be used more judiciously in package recipes." Unfortunately, the reality is that any executable that has dynamic library dependencies in conda is not symlink-suitable, and since symlinks really only make sense cross-filesystem, hard links are really the only suitable solution.

As a result, I suspect conda is not generally usable across filesystems for many packages unless the `--copy` argument to `conda create` is used (which is, unfortunately, slow). A (e.g.) `--softlink-except-executables` (that would be the default if the package and env filesystem are not the same) would make sense for this case and be slightly faster than `--copy`.

I investigated this and found that there is a reason for the password prompt:

The code establishes a notification listener for the OS message notifications. In order to do that, a password is needed for logging on to the Stomp connection (see `NotificationReceiver.__init__()`). Because the `zhmc` command was used with a session stored in the environment, a password was not available and needed to be prompted for.

If the `zhmc partition console` command is executed with the `-p` option instead of relying on the `ZHMC_SESSION` env var, that password is used and no password prompt occurs.

I believe it is not possible to use a saved session ID with Stomp, but it deserves further investigation to be sure.

1. Libindy 1.5 allows efficient search of entities in wallet. It makes secure implementation of wallet protocol very complex and forces big probability of vulnerabilities in custom wallet implementations.
2. Community decided to allow usage only one wallet that is shipped with libindy. You can see corresponded accepted HIPE here: https://github.com/hyperledger/indy-hipe/blob/master/text/0013-wallets/README.md
3. Libindy walllet allows to plug different storages to cover different use cases. See HIPE and design https://github.com/hyperledger/indy-sdk/tree/master/doc/design/003-wallet-storage for details. Storage is responsible only for data access and search, but doesn't cover any encryption. 
4. In our vision storages should be implemented as plugins to libindy and not as part of applications written in application language to allow usage of storage code for applications written in different languages. See https://github.com/hyperledger/indy-sdk/tree/master/doc/design/006-cli-plugins

As result we don't have short-term plans to expose indy_register_wallet_storage as part of any wrappers interface as the most of applications will call plugin_init API provided by plugin instead of calling indy_register_wallet_storage directly. 

If you think that you still need this functionality you can create ticket in JIRA and describe your use case. We are also welcome for contribution. I suggest also consider usage of default SQLite based storage for iOS agents use cases as it was designed to cover this.

It seems like tox is leaving the compiled .so files around in the main BTrees directory...and since that's on the sys.path as described above (because it's the working directory) we get this problem. The `cPersistence` module wasn't even compiled because of PURE_PYTHON, but the C BTree modules were, *and* the BTree modules don't respect the runtime PURE_PYTHON variable like persistent does. So we attempt to load the C BTree module, which uses the Capsule API to load `persistent.cPersistence.CAPI`...and I'm guessing it just takes the AttributeError exception and doesn't alter it into an ImportError.

So maybe a better fix would be to have the BTreeModuleTemplate.c look for the NULL return and raise an ImportError explicitly? That would seem to fix the root cause.

(I may have some of the exact details wrong, this is my first time looking at the way those modules interact at the C level.)

Or we could just change the tox working directory to fix the test and say that having BTree C code but no persistent C code (or vice versa) just isn't supported...which, it really isn't. As it stands, if the PURE_PYTHON variable is set, but the extensions are both available, Python code that subclasses Persistent is going to get a different thing than the BTree code is doing.

`from memote.suite.api import test_model
import pandas as pd
from future.types.newdict import newdict

# Automatically load your models into a list here.
model_list = [model]

for counter, model in enumerate(model_list):

# This is the API command that runs the test
    report_dictionary = memote.test_model(model, results=True)[1]['report']

    super_categories = []
    test_ids = []
    final_results = []

    for i in report_dictionary.keys():
        for j in report_dictionary[i].keys():
            test_ids.append(j)
            result = report_dictionary[i][j]
            super_categories.append(i)
# Some results are currently stored in long lists containing several datatypes
# For readability we only display the lenght of lists larger than 2 items.
            if type(result) == list and len(result) > 2:
                final_results.append(len(result))
# Some results are actually pandas.dataframes exported as dictionaries.
# Here we just calculate a summary of the values from these dictionaries.
            elif type(result) == newdict:
                summary_dict = {x: sum(1 for b in y if not b) for x, y in
                                result.items() if x != 'index'}
                final_results.append(summary_dict)
            else:
                final_results.append(result)

# With the first model we generate the structure of the dataframe
    if counter == 0:
        data = {'Category': pd.Series(super_categories),
                'Test ID': pd.Series(test_ids),
                '{}'.format(model.id): pd.Series(final_results)}
        df = pd.DataFrame(data)
# For every additional model we'll add a column
    else:
        df['{}'.format(model.id)] = pd.Series(final_results)

# With the dataframe you can now group/sort etc to analyse the models' performance
df
`

Hi @maelp , I think you are referencing the RIR creation method in [this paper](https://static.googleusercontent.com/media/research.google.com/en//pubs/archive/46107.pdf) (ref [1] in the one you linked). Essentially, these guys are rediscovering 60+ years of signal processing on their own. I wouldn't recommend this as a reference for your own implementation.

In their method, they need to generate the RIR at a higher frequency because they use impulses that are rounded to the nearest sample. Pyroomacoustics doesn't have this problem because we generate _fractional delays_ directly. No rounding to the nearest sample is ever done. What comes out of the simulator is better than what you'll get using this upsampling method.

Regarding computations, OLA is a completely standard method. The STFT method of pyroomacoustics lets you do it. Although it is a good point that this is not actually what we use to convolve the RIR in the room simulation. Instead we use `scipy.signal.fftconvolve` which uses FFT for the convolution (_Real FFT filtering_ in the paper linked, Eq 4). So from their paper, an efficient implementation of OLA might be 2x as fast. The problem for us is that the OLA in `stft` is done in python, so it might be actually slower than `fftconvolve`. This should be checked.

> 
> Is there a better approach?

Hi!
1. Yeah, it's just for parsing. My app sometimes has to check what was the cause of the error and the error codes is already there. So I think it would be great to have them parsed.
2. No, it's just refactoring that give nothing specific, but improved readability (yes, it's subjective):
 a) it's easier to access to individual fields (just by doing `_fields[field_name]`), e.g when you need the type of the field and you have just a name. 
  b) it's easier to iterate:
       `for field in self._fields:` iterates over the field names
       `list(self._fields)` gets the list of field names.
  Of course I thought of simple `dict` at first, but we need to save the order of fields, so `OrderedDict` is here, which, as I suppose, perfectly fits such a use case.

@hitbra: The validations have been helpful to me because they let me build templates that have fewer problems when I use them to stand up infrastructure. I agree with you that there should only be validations that provide value.

I am confused about the difference between **RoleName** and **PhysicalResourceId**. The documentation clearly says that the limit of the **RoleName** is 64 [see http://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/APIReference/API_Role.html ]. When creating a role through the web GUI, one is presented with the option to set the **RoleName**. When one does it via CloudFormation, then the resource name becomes the **RoleName** and **PhysicalResourceId** minus a dash and the unique ID (14 characters). I can't find in the CloudFormation documents if there is a way to set the **RoleName** without the unique ID.

Let's assume that this is the intended behavior: all IAM roles will have a name that is up to 64 characters long where 14 characters are chosen by the standing up process. This leaves 50 characters that someone writing a CloudFormation template can use. Any more than this and some of it will be lost.

I think the validation can be helpful at this point. If I have roles that only differ after 50 characters, then they will look the same after they get truncated. For instance:

* `'VeryLongNameAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAJoseSmith'`
* `'VeryLongNameAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAKosSmith'`

would become

* `'VeryLongNameAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA-1P3793NZ4KTJO`
* `'VeryLongNameAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA-3WS793NZ4KTJO`

These two names are hard to distinguish. A validation in troposphere can help prevent this. 

As to your point, that you don't want validation forced upon you, I agree that if a reasonable usage can be devised, it would sometimes be desirable to turn off these checks.

As I know, the chunked body is a spec about logical chunk, not about TCP packet fragment.

A question here: does httptools expect to feed the whole HTTP body (at least "a chunk") at same time? Then it can be a user fault - but still weird.

Because httptools is the parser, I think basically the users can't determine which part of http request is going to httptools or not. For the point of view of user, "end of chunk" of http body and any fragmented packet in http header is not recognizable before putting it into the parser.

I haven't actually tried that particular test code, but I was more referring to building actual Python extension modules.  When trying to build a simple extension module, the link fails with undefined symbol errors on Linux.  That is- when I don't link to any libpython.

The test project in this PR is based on my experiments [here](https://github.com/opadron/weak-linking-demo).  There is a shared lib, L; a module lib, M; and the executable, E.  E is linked against L.  E also dynamically loads M.  And M, itself uses code from L, but the main question is whether M should be linked against L.  This test was supposed to emulate a typical example of building a python extension module.  As an aside, I haven't been able to build M without linking it against L on Linux, I run into the exact same problems as I did with actual Python extension modules.

I figure besides library version mismatches, the only other potential source of problems with linking for both the module and the executable is that it may result in code duplication.  If the library had some shared state, we should be able to observe the effects of such duplication by inspecting the shared state from the perspectives of E and M, and then comparing.  If there is code duplication, the counting routines from each would have their own counters instead of sharing.  If the loader coalesces the duplicate symbols, then they would share, which is the desired behavior.  This is what I check for under the "SYMBOL_DEDUPING" name.

I'm going to check to see if I can get your simpler test code to compile and link.  If so, then there's clearly something about how linking on Linux works that I'm not privy to.

for examle:
```
model = Sequential()
model.add(Flatten(input_shape=(1,) + (2,)))

config = {
    'class_name': model.__class__.__name__,
    'config': model.get_config(),
}

```

Then in the actor __init__ function:
`model = model_from_config(model_config, custom_objects={})`

The model is been serialized only once. When the agents are rolling out, only the weights will be shared between different agents.

It is in principle possible to run multi-threaded on the CPU, but performance can be memory bound so that a naive parallel loop over the pixels doesn't seem to work very well.   At the moment each transform runs in a single thread, but you can run multiple images in parallel for batch processing.  See:
https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/master/demo/batch_processing.py

You might also look at [PDWT](https://github.com/pierrepaleo/PDWT) which is implemented in Python + CUDA.  It uses wavelet coefficients borrowed from PyWavelets and supports 1D and 2D transforms on the GPU. For large enough images the GPU implementation should be faster than PyWavelets. For small images the overhead of transferring data to/from the GPU will make it slower.

The [libdwt](https://github.com/xbarin02/libdwt) library and associated publications have compared a number of potential CPU implementations exploiting multi-threading in combination with SIMD. That library is not in Python and is more limited in the range of wavelets and transforms supported, but should have higher performance.  However, this comes at cost of substantially increased complexity in the implementation.

> Doesn't pywavelets ultimately rely on numpy?

Although PyWavelets does rely on numpy, the bulk of the computation is done by C code for down-sample d or up-sampled convolutions that is included with PyWavelets itself. C code is in:
https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/tree/master/pywt/_extensions/c
and Cython code in
https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/tree/master/pywt/_extensions

Numpy itself does not have GPU support.  Some other libraries such as [CuPy](https://github.com/cupy/cupy) and [PyCUDA](tps://mathema.tician.de/software/pycuda/) provide numpy-like GPU arrays.  You cannot pass these GPU arrays to any of the functions in PyWavelets, though.

So you removed the softmax in criteria and your model is returning probabilities directly? The problem with that is that gradient calculation will be wrong in that case. It might not matter for decision-based attacks, but the remaining attacks will not work correctly when your model returns probabilities instead of logits.
In other words: it's not as simple as removing the softmax in the criteria; to calculate and differentiate the cross-entropy in a numerically stable way, the logits are needed.

In Keras and TensorFlow it's relatively easy to access the logits even if the model outputs probabilities without having to modify the model (and in fact for Keras we even have built-in support for this because there it's common to have a model that outputs probabilities), but I am not sure if this is possible in PyTorch.
If this (i.e. accessing the input tensor of another tensor, e.g. traversing the graph backwards from the probabilities-tensor to the logits tensor) is possible in PyTorch, it would be a great addition to properly support your use-case.

I have a temporary workaround for the problem.
Setting up a global timeout for socket calls allows the `self.read` call to fail, and the API can then make another try immediately.
Adding this at the beginning of the script will do that:
```
import socket
socket.setdefaulttimeout(30)
```

This is of course not ideal as this will apply to _any_ HTTP calls made in the script, but that should be a useful workaround in a lot of cases.

Currently, only Formatters are supported (because this is a well-defined extension-point and there is a convincing need for it). As long as the problem can be solved without additional plugin-points (or "hooks"), I would first try to use it (because I do not see the selling point (yet)). With the ``before_all()`` (and using ``userdata`` as configuration mechanism) you should be able to inject most plugins with very few lines of code (best case: 1 line).

If you had something else in mind, we would need to discuss where you see the need for extension-points in the behave run and why.

Assume:
- Input feature maps: XH x XW x XC x N
- Filters: FH x FW x XC x FC 
- Strides: strides[0] and strides[1] are row (h) and col (w) stride, respectively.

For the **SAME** convolution:  the output height (YH) and width (YW) are computed as:

- YH = ceil(float(H) / float(strides[0]))
- YW  = ceil(float(W) / float(strides[1]))

and the paddings are computed as:

- pad_along_height = max((YH - 1) * strides[0] + FH - XH, 0)
- pad_along_width = max((YW - 1) * strides[1] + FW - XW, 0)
- pad_top = pad_along_height / 2
- pad_bottom = pad_along_height - pad_top
- pad_left = pad_along_width / 2
- pad_right = pad_along_width - pad_left


For the **VALID** convolution:  the output height (YH) and width (YW) are computed as:

- YH = ceil(float(XH - FH + 1) / float(strides[0]))
- YW  = ceil(float(XW - FW + 1) / float(strides[1]))

and the paddings are always zeros.

Think about when stride is 1, the distinction:

**SAME**: output size is the same with input size. To do so, one needs to pad the input so the filter can sweep outside of the input maps
**VALID**: output size shrinks by filter_size - 1, and the filters always sweep at valid positions inside the input maps. No padding needed.

@mpaget @petewa The only thing we removed is 3D ingestion, as in combining 2D ingest with stacking in one step. Reasons for this removal were outlined by @omad in the pull request. Basically incremental ingestion in the presence of stacking is poorly defined. Nothing stops anyone implementing a version of ingest+stack in one step, it just should not happen in the `datacube ingest` command, not until incremental ingestion for this configuration is implemented in a robust fashion.

"Virtual products" have nothing todo with the changes in this pull request. 3d raster building already exists, but constrained to just loading data from one product without any transform. Virtual products are meant to generalise VRT to include more products than one and to incorporate on-the-fly processing that can happen one time slice at a time.

3D data **reads** can not be removed, because they don't exist. The abstraction for reader is per timeslice per band, nothing in this pull request changes that.

If you have 3D reads from S3 storage, great, nothing should be affected, I assume you are doing it outside of `dc.load` since abstraction within `dc.load` is to read one timeslice for one band, and I haven't seen any changes in any branch that attempt to change this behaviour. You will need to re-create 3D ingest by copying old code into a standalone app, as a quick work-around you can use `datacube ingest` from an older checkout to ingest your data and continue working on read/execute in the develop branch.

According to [distutils](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/distutils/dist.py#L339-L343) there's _three_ places this config file can butt it's head in and ruin stuff:

        There are three possible config files: distutils.cfg in the
        Distutils installation directory (ie. where the top-level
        Distutils __inst__.py file lives), a file in the user's home
        directory named .pydistutils.cfg on Unix and pydistutils.cfg
        on Windows/Mac; and setup.cfg in the current directory.

```
pydistutils = pydistutils_cfg()
```

Agreed, that would indeed be loss of recent state in this design. And since the uwsgi master process will much too happily `kill -9` workers that are past their prime - dataloss will be guaranteed in that case if the collector did not sweep in soon enough.

To me that comes to the trade off of sending metrics somewhere external immediately while handling an incoming request (to guarantee survivability of metrics, but adding latency), or to buffer up measurements within each worker-process for a configurable short time, and gather the values in bulk every X seconds. All in-memory queueing means the potential for dataloss, but at much greater throughput. Adding additional communication complexity to the master/worker hierarchy as #66 does seems like it might be a good tradeoff, but things do become more difficult to reason about.
